steps.title: Dermatology
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Tertiary Care

steps.title: Atopic Dermatitis / Eczema
steps.text: Which topical corticosteroid regimen is appropriate for a child with moderate/severe atopic dermatitis at the tertiary care level?
steps.rationale: The guideline specifies medium–high potency steroids for affected areas, but low potency only for the face, neck, and folds.

textChoices.text: Low-potency TCS twice daily on all body areas including the face
Medium- to high-potency TCS on affected areas (excluding face, neck, and skin folds) for 2–4 weeks
High-potency TCS applied continuously for more than 4 weeks
Only emollients should be used; steroids are avoided at this level

steps.title: Atopic Dermatitis / Eczema
steps.text: Which of the following is a recommended next step if a patient with atopic dermatitis does not respond to standard topical therapy?
steps.rationale: Refractory disease should trigger specialist consultation and possibly escalated therapy options.

textChoices.text: Refer the patient for cosmetic skin therapy
Consult a dermatologist and consider topical calcineurin inhibitors or systemic therapy
Discontinue all medications and monitor for spontaneous remission

steps.title: Atopic Dermatitis / Eczema
steps.text: What is the role of phototherapy in tertiary care for atopic dermatitis?
steps.rationale: UV phototherapy is used as a non-pharmacological option for refractory cases in tertiary settings.

textChoices.text: It is the first-line treatment in mild disease
It is used routinely in children under 2 years
It is an option for refractory moderate/severe cases

steps.title: Atopic Dermatitis / Eczema
steps.text: In severe cases of atopic dermatitis that do not respond to topical agents, what systemic treatment approach is recommended?
steps.rationale: Systemic immunomodulators (non-corticosteroid) may be considered when the condition is severe and refractory.

textChoices.text: Systemic corticosteroids as long-term maintenance therapy
No systemic treatment should be considered

steps.title: Miliaria
steps.text: What is the first-line approach to managing miliaria in children?
steps.rationale: Reducing heat and sweat is the cornerstone of treatment, as miliaria is caused by sweat duct blockage and irritation.

textChoices.text: Minimize exposure to heat and humidity
Use antifungal cream on affected areas

steps.title: Miliaria
steps.text: What should caregivers be reassured about when managing a typical case of miliaria?
steps.rationale: Miliaria is typically self-limiting and resolves quickly with environmental adjustments.

textChoices.text: The condition requires long-term topical steroid use
It is likely to leave permanent scarring
It usually resolves within 2 days after changing to a cooler environment
It requires specialist referral in all cases

steps.title: Miliaria
steps.text: Which of the following topical treatments is recommended in the management of miliaria?
steps.rationale: Calamine lotion is commonly used for its soothing, cooling effect and helps relieve itching and irritation in miliaria.

steps.title: Miliaria
steps.text: When should a child with miliaria be referred to a dermatologist?
steps.rationale: Most cases resolve with basic care; referral is indicated only if symptoms persist.

textChoices.text: If the rash spreads to the trunk
If the condition persists despite conservative management
If the child is younger than 6 months
As soon as the diagnosis is made

steps.title: Urticaria
steps.text: In a child with severe anaphylaxis and bronchospasm not responding to IM epinephrine, which of the following is the recommended next step?
steps.rationale: Salbutamol is indicated if bronchospasm persists despite IM epinephrine.

textChoices.text: Start salbutamol nebulizer 2.5–5 mg, repeat every 15 minutes if needed

steps.title: Urticaria
steps.text: Which of the following H1 antihistamines can be used intravenously in the management of severe anaphylaxis?
steps.rationale: IV diphenhydramine is one of the tertiary-level antihistamine options for rapid relief of symptoms.

steps.title: Urticaria
steps.text: What is the next step in managing refractory anaphylaxis if a child is not responding to repeated IM adrenaline?
steps.rationale: For refractory shock, continuous adrenaline infusion is indicated and may need to be titrated to maintain perfusion.

textChoices.text: Start an adrenaline infusion at 0.1–1 mcg/kg/min, titrated to effect
Intubate and discharge once airway is secure

steps.title: Urticaria
steps.text: When is ICU admission indicated for a child with anaphylaxis?
steps.rationale: ICU admission is required if airway control is necessary or if there’s persistent shock despite treatment.

textChoices.text: For every case of mild urticaria
Only if more than three antihistamines are used
If there is airway compromise requiring intubation

steps.title: Psoriasis
steps.text: What is the recommended initial step in the management of any child presenting with suspected psoriasis?
steps.rationale: Proper assessment of disease extent (BSA) guides the choice between topical and systemic therapy.

textChoices.text: Assess disease severity using estimated body surface area (BSA) affected
Perform a skin biopsy to confirm diagnosis

steps.title: Psoriasis
steps.text: Which of the following is an appropriate first-line treatment for mild psoriasis (BSA <3–5%)?
steps.rationale: Mild psoriasis is typically managed with topical agents such as corticosteroids, calcipotriene, or retinoids.

steps.title: Psoriasis
steps.text: Which treatment is appropriate for moderate to severe psoriasis (BSA >3–5%)?
steps.rationale: Systemic pharmacotherapy and phototherapy are recommended for more extensive disease.

textChoices.text: Systemic therapy and/or narrowband UV-B phototherapy

steps.title: Psoriasis
steps.text: What must be done for all suspected cases of psoriasis, regardless of severity?
steps.rationale: The guideline clearly states that all suspected psoriasis cases must be discussed with dermatology specialists.

textChoices.text: Start antibiotics to rule out bacterial causes
Discuss the case with the dermatology department

steps.title: Oculocutaneous Albinism
steps.text: Which of the following best describes the correct use of sunscreen for someone with oculocutaneous albinism?
steps.rationale: Frequent application of sunscreen with at least SPF 30 is crucial for UV protection in albinism.

textChoices.text: Apply sunscreen once in the morning only
Use sunscreen only on cloudy days
Apply sunscreen with SPF 15 every 6 hours
Apply SPF 30 or higher liberally every 2 hours when in the sun

steps.title: Oculocutaneous Albinism
steps.text: Which of the following medications should be avoided or used cautiously in patients with albinism due to increased photosensitivity?
steps.rationale: Medications such as tetracyclines, NSAIDs, and some diuretics can increase photosensitivity and should be avoided when possible.

steps.title: Oculocutaneous Albinism
steps.text: How often should children with oculocutaneous albinism undergo routine skin examination?
steps.rationale: Regular skin checks beginning in adolescence help detect early signs of skin cancer.

steps.title: Oculocutaneous Albinism
steps.text: What should caregivers of children with albinism be educated to look for during skin self-exams?
steps.rationale: Awareness of changes like non-healing or symptomatic lesions helps in early detection of skin cancer.

textChoices.text: New or changing lesions, especially in sun-exposed areas
Normal freckling on the arms and legs

steps.title: Oculocutaneous Albinism
steps.text: When a child with oculocutaneous albinism is referred for optometry or ophthalmology evaluation, what is the most likely purpose of the assessment?
steps.rationale: Children with albinism often have visual impairments due to nystagmus, refractive errors, or foveal hypoplasia—optometry/ophthalmology helps improve functional vision.

textChoices.text: To evaluate for laser treatment of retinal vessels
To correct refractive errors and provide low vision aids
